The Royal Commonwealth Society for the Blind, under its previous name of British Empire Society for the Blind, was set up as an independent corporation in January 1950, following the report of a joint Committee of the Colonial Office and the National Institute for the Blind entitled "Blindness in British African and Middle East Territories". Its objects are to stimulate official and voluntary action and to take the lead in a movement "to promote the welfare, education and employment of the blind, and to prevent blindness". Its area of operation originally included any British Colonies not possessing responsible government, British Protectorates, Protected States, United Kingdom Trust Territories and the High Commission Territories of South Africa, but provision was made for this area to be extended by the Executive Council of the Society to include British territories possessing responsible government, and territories administered under a Condominium to which the British Government is a party. Its area was further extended in 1957 to enable the Society not only to continue its work in former colonial territories which have become members of the British Commonwealth but also to empower the Society to undertake central activities for the benefit of organizations for the blind throughout the Commonwealth. The Society is under the direction of a Council to which three members are appointed by the Secretary of State for the Colonies.

Since the Society's inception, local organizations for the blind have been established in 33 territories, Regional Officers have been stationed in East, West and Central Africa, the West Indies and Mediterranean territories, and ophthalmic surveys have been conducted in East, Central and West Africa. Other activities in which the Society has assisted have been the establishment of some 60 schools and training centres, registration of the blind, Braille production, and training in welfare work amongst the blind. It is also working with Governments and organizations in various territories in the prevention of blindness, in the experimental development of new types of training for blind farmers, peasant cultivators and village craftsmen, and in projects of 'open' education whereby blind children are educated in ordinary primary schools.

It has established a Commonwealth Scholarship Fund to assist those wishing to obtain special qualifications in ophthalmology, to help defray the cost of training blind welfare workers and to provide financial assistance in other deserving cases, e.g. to meet the cost of attendance at a useful international conference or to finance a publication or investigation.

The Royal Over-Seas League was founded in 1910 by Sir Evelyn Wrench to promote friendship and understanding between the peoples of the British Commonwealth, and to maintain its traditions by individual service. Membership is open to all British subjects and Commonwealth citizens.

The League conducts various Commonwealth Essay Competitions, maintains a special interest in looking after Commonwealth music students in London, is keenly interested in migration and publishes a quarterly journal Overseas .

At World Headquarters first-class residential club facilities and bedroom accommodation for about 100 members are available. In addition, accommodation for about 40 members is available at the Beresford House Annexe, 1 St. James's Place, a short distance from World Headquarters. This accommodation can be obtained for a longer period than is normally possible at Over-Seas House. A travel and theatre bureau is provided for members, and a special secretarial service for overseas businessmen who are members is also available, including accommodation and secretarial assistance for conferences. A comprehensive programme of social and cultural activities is arranged for the benefit of members and their friends.
Accommodation is also available at Edinburgh, Glasgow and Belfast.

Since the International Geophysical Year more and more countries have become actively interested in the Antarctic. This increase in international activity has led to a review of the arrangements for providing scientific advice to the British Antarctic Survey, by which name the former Falkland Islands Dependencies Survey is now known.

In June 1962 the Falkland Islands Dependencies Scientific Committee was disbanded and its commitments vested in the Royal Society's National Committee on Antarctic Research. Its terms of reference are:—
To advise the High Commissioner, British Antarctic Territory, through the Director, British Antarctic Survey, on:—

(i) Scientific aspects of the annual programme of work of the British Antarctic Survey as proposed by the High Commissioner of the British Antarctic Territory; (ii) scientific aspects of any other proposals or activities arising from the British occupation of British Antarctic Territory; (iii) the use to be made of scientific specimens and data accumulated during previous season's work by the Survey and to be accumulated in the future.

The School was established in 1916 by Royal Charter and its purposes are to further research in, and to extend the study and knowledge of, the languages of Eastern and African peoples ancient and modern and the literature, history, religion, law, customs and art of those peoples. The Governing Body of the School includes inter alia a member appointed by the Secretary of State for the Colonies and members nominated for appointment by certain Commonwealth Governments who are Contributing Bodies.

The School is organized in ten Departments, viz., Anthropology and Sociology; Economic and Political Studies; History; Law; Phonetics and Linguistics; and Languages and Cultures of: India, Pakistan and Ceylon; South-East Asia and the Islands; Far East; Near and Middle East; Africa. Instruction is provided for first and higher degrees of the University of London in many of the principal languages and cultures of Asia and Africa. Many short courses of instruction for students not wishing to read for degrees are also provided. Language instruction is provided for Cadets of the Overseas Civil Service in those languages for which there is no provision at the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge; and also for those Cadets whose Governments require them to take the short intensive course of preliminary training instead of the full sessional course. Courses are also provided for other members of the Overseas Civil Service home on leave and for members of many Government Departments. Special provision may be made for members of commercial and industrial firms about to proceed to the East or to Africa.
Publications of the School are The Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies , the Calendar and the Annual Report of the Governing Body .

The Inter-University Council for Higher Education Overseas (entitled until 1955 The Inter-University Council for Higher Education in the Colonies) was constituted in 1946 at the invitation of the Secretary of State, by the universities of the United Kingdom and of the Colonial territories. The Council consists of one representative of each of these universities, co-opted members and the Educational Adviser to the Secretary for Technical Co-operation.

Its general purposes, as recommended by the Asquith Commission on Higher Education in the Colonies, are (a) to strengthen co-operation between the universities of the United Kingdom and the existing universities in Colonial territories; (b) to foster the development of higher colleges in the Colonies and their advance to university status; and (c) to take such other action as will give effect to the principles recommended by the Commission as appropriate for the promotion of higher education, learning and research in the Colonies.

In 1954 the Council accepted an invitation from the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations to extend its services to assist the development of higher education in the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland. The Council has continued to assist university institutions in Ghana, Malaya and Nigeria since those countries achieved independence. From the start it was specially invited to extend its assistance to the Sudan, and has continued to do so.

A Colonial Liaison Section was established at the Building Research Station in 1948. It was expanded in 1953 to deal with planning and housing matters. A Tropical Paint Research Fellow was attached in 1957. The Division was renamed in 1958. The greater part of the cost is contributed by research funds made available by the Department of Technical Co-operation. The main functions of the Division are:
(i) To collect from and supply to overseas territories information on building, housing and planning matters including that on relevant developments in the United Kingdom and elsewhere;
(ii) to assist with the solution of specific technical problems and to answer enquiries on building and housing;
(iii) to advise on building, housing and planning policy in overseas territories.

Information is disseminated through Overseas Building Notes ; Notes on Building, Housing and Planning in Tropical and Sub-Tropical Countries ; and by occasional monographs Tropical Building Studies , published by Her Majesty's Stationery Office.

Developments in the overseas territories called for review of the advisory machinery, and the Medical Research Council put forward proposals to meet the changing circumstances. The proposals were

TROPICAL PRODUCTS INSTITUTE (formerly Colonial Products Laboratory)
56-62 Gray's Inn Road, London, W.C.1.
Telephone: CHancery 5412-9.

The Institute has its roots in two bodies, namely the Plant and Animal Products Department of the Imperial Institute and the Colonial Products Research Council. The former of these two bodies came into existence in 1893 to provide a scientific advisory service dealing with the plant and animal products of the British Empire. The Colonial Products Research Council was set up in 1942 to advise the Secretary of State for the Colonies as to the more fundamental research that should be conducted on the renewable resources of Colonial territories to help bring about their greater use.

In 1953 the work of these two bodies was combined and in 1957 the organization moved from the Imperial Institute Building to its present premises, whereupon the name Tropical Products Institute was adopted.
By agreement with the Lord President of the Council, the Tropical Products Institute became a station of the Department of Scientific and Industrial Research with effect from 1st April 1959.

The Tropical Products Institute is charged with the investigation of, and provision of information about, the renewable resources of tropical and sub-tropical countries, particularly British Colonial and Commonwealth Territories.

Laboratory examinations are made and information supplied on plant and animal products such as essential oils, spices, gums and resins, oils and oilseeds, waxes, foods and feeding-stuffs, drugs and insecticides of vegetable origin, fruit and fruit products, vegetables, fish, fibres, pulp, and board making and papermaking materials. Information is also supplied about sources of raw materials, cultural conditions, methods of production, marketing and commercial possibilities of products. Specifications and estimates of cost are obtained for plant, machinery and equipment which may be required for production and processing. Commercial valuations and assessments of the suitability of materials are also obtained and factory tests are arranged when necessary.

In addition to laboratory investigations arising out of samples received for examination, the Institute conducts more fundamental studies of tropical plant and animal products with a view to discovering materials which might form the basis of new industries, and facts about existing products or by-products which might benefit industries already in operation. It is particularly concerned at present with studies on medicinal plants, surface active compounds from sugar, constituents of banana skins, the properties of certain fixed vegetable oils, and the production of chipboard from timbers and tropical waste materials.

The Institute's Economics Section is concerned with the various economic problems arising from existing, or in the development of new, industries. It conducts market surveys and collects statistical information about tropical plant and animal products and works with other sections of the Institute on the economic aspects of enquiries received.

The development work of the Institute involves selecting from work conducted in the Institute or elsewhere (Universities, etc.), ideas which might beneficially be developed to production scale in tropical areas. Where appropriate pilot scale examination of the selected projects is carried out and if results are favourable full scale production in tropical regions is encouraged.

UNIVERSITY OF THE WEST INDIES, FACULTY OF AGRICULTURE (formerly Imperial College of Tropical Agriculture)

Secretary's Address: St. Augustine, Trinidad, The West Indies. Telegrams: Stomata, Port of Spain, Trinidad. Vice-Principal —P. M. Sherlock, C.B.E. Secretary —V. A. A. Archer. Professor of Botany —J. W. Purseglove. Professor of Agriculture —P. N. Wilson. Dean of Faculty and Professor of Zoology —G. L. Underwood. Head of Soil Science and Chemistry Department —(Vacant).

The College was established in 1921, as the outcome of recommendations made in the Report of the Tropical Agricultural College Committee appointed by Viscount Milner in 1919. It was incorporated by Royal Charter in 1926.

The main objects of the Faculty are to provide instruction, and to conduct research, in tropical agriculture. It is the recognised centre in the British Commonwealth for post-graduate training in tropical agriculture and the agricultural sciences for officers destined to serve in the Colonial Agricultural Service. Caribbean agriculture is taught at undergraduate level.

As from 1st August 1960 the Imperial College of Tropical Agriculture was merged with the University College of the West Indies and became its Faculty of Agriculture. Following on this merger a three-year course leading to a pass B.Sc. degree in agriculture was instituted in October 1960. The University College became a full University in 1962.

Almost all Colonial Governments and the United Kingdom Government contribute to the income of the Faculty of Agriculture. The University is also responsible for the administration of the Regional Research Centre for the British territories within the Caribbean area. These are financed in whole, or in part, by Her Majesty's Treasury from Colonial Development and Welfare funds and by industry. The buildings, at St. Augustine, near St. Joseph, Trinidad, are on land granted by the Government of Trinidad and Tobago.
The academic year extends from the beginning of October to the end of June. The following courses and facilities for study are available:

1. Post-Graduate Courses. —Diploma in Tropical Agriculture—Open to holders of a degree or diploma of any British university, university college or other academic institution specifically approved by the Governing Body of the Imperial College of Tropical Agriculture. Special facilities are available for such graduates to extend their knowledge of, or undertake investigations into, subjects pertaining to tropical agriculture.
Candidates are also accepted for the M.Sc. and Ph.D of the University of the West Indies.
2. Special Courses. —Open to officers of agricultural departments in the tropics, or similar institutions. Details are arranged on application.
3. A Three-Year Degree Course in agriculture open to candidates satisfying the entry requirements of the University.
The tuition fees are as follows:—
(a) Post-Graduate Course £75 per academic year.
(b) Special Course £75 per academic year.
(c) Degree Course £60 per academic year.

The Women's Corona Society is a voluntary and non-political association of women of all races. Originally its members were all connected with Her Majesty's Overseas Civil Service and the public services in Colonial territories, but it is now open to any women in active sympathy with the aims of the Society.

These are: to be a link between its members throughout the Commonwealth; to be of service to them and, through them, to the wider community, with particular emphasis on helping to promote friendship between the people of the Commonwealth. In Britain this is done by giving help to members and other women going overseas or visiting the United Kingdom, not only by the Headquarters Office in London but also in Scotland, West of England and Oxford where groups have been formed. If those going overseas are too far from any of these centres, Headquarters will put them in touch by post with a member on leave from the territory in which they are interested and will supply them with up-to-date information on living conditions overseas.

Further help over problems of living in a new country is provided by three-day conferences held in May and October, and shorter ones in the intervening months. These meetings, for which no charge is made, are open to anyone who may be interested; besides lectures and films, they provide a chance for women of many territories to meet informally. For overseas visitors hospitality is arranged in members' homes as well as classes in cookery etc., and escorts are provided for sight-seeing and shopping. For members there are other social occasions such as the annual dinner and garden party, lunch meetings and evening parties, which serve as a link between those who carry on the work of the Society, including services such as the meeting of and looking after children in transit through London; the compiling of information about holiday homes for children and a register of accommodation.

A Newsletter is circulated twice a year reporting the activities of the Society in Britain and the forty Corona Bodies overseas where local representatives welcome newcomers, helping them to get to know the women of the country and take part with them in giving voluntary service wherever there is most need.
The subscription is 10s. a year or £4 for life membership. For further particulars, apply to the Organizing Secretary.

MALTA
STATE OF MALTA, G.C.
Area

The total area is 122 square miles made up of the three main islands as follows : Malta 94.876 square miles, Gozo 25.899 square miles and Comino 1.075 square miles. Filfla and Cominotto are mere islets.
Population
The population of Malta is European; on the 31st December 1961 the total population was estimated to be 329,763 divided as follows: Malta 302,336, Gozo and Comino 27,427.
It is the policy of the Government to encourage and assist emigration. From 1946 to 1961, 80,383 persons emigrated; the figure for 1961 was 3,580.

Geographical Features

Malta consists of a plateau with a hard core of coralline limestone on the west side which descends by gentle steps to a plain composed of a thick stratum of soft globigerine limestone on the south-east. The highest point on the Island, situated near Dingli, is just over 800 feet above sea level. The Grand Harbour and Marsamxett Harbour are excellent ports of call, while safe anchorages are also provided by Marsaxlokk Bay, St. Paul's Bay and Mellieha Bay. There are no rivers, lakes or forests. In low-lying places the soil, which contains a high percentage of lime and is rich in phosphates, is deep, but elsewhere it is shallow. Cultivation is mainly in terraced fields where the soil is held by rubble walls.
Climate

The climate is healthy. There are no excessive periods of hot or cold weather. The lowest temperature in 1961 was 43.8° F. recorded on the 14th January, while the highest, 94.2° F., occurred on the 10th September. The mean temperature for 1961 was 66.8° F. and the mean annual rainfall was 10.54 inches. The mean annual rainfall at Valletta for the past ten years is 19.11 inches. Rain is rare in June, July and August. In August the heat at sea level approaches that experienced in subtropical countries. The nights, however, are pleasant and cool, except during the prevalence of the scirocco wind which blows from the south-east.
History

From Roman times until 1530 Malta was subjected successively to Arab and Sicilian domination. In 1530, the Emperor Charles V gave the Island to the Knights of St. John of Jerusalem, who held it until 1798, when Napoleon Bonaparte invested it on his way to Egypt,

having prepared the ground by intrigue and revolutionary propaganda. On 12 June 1798 Ferdinand de Hompesch, the last Grand Master in Malta, capitulated without any resistance and the Order was dispersed. The French installed themselves in the islands, but their interference with Maltese laws and customs brought about a sharp reaction, and the Maltese drove the French troops within the fortified towns and, with the help of the British fleet, held them closely blockaded for two years, after which the French, reduced to extremities, surrendered to the British.

In 1800 the Government was placed in the hands of Great Britain. The Treaty of Amiens provided that the islands should be restored to the Knights, but this was prevented by the recommencement of the war. The representatives of the Maltese people, freely elected, unanimously approved a declaration of rights in which, inter alia , they requested the protection of His Majesty the King of Great Britain and Ireland, recognised him as their Sovereign Lord, whilst maintaining that the power to establish the Constitution of the Islands, which His Majesty was bound to keep inviolate, was vested in the General Congress elected by the people.

Malta was a vital point in allied strategy in the Second World War and the Island was subjected to heavy enemy air attacks between the entry of Italy into the war in 1940 and the liberation of North Africa in 1943. In 1942, Malta was awarded the George Cross in recognition of the part she had played in the War.
Constitution

After the war Malta was granted a measure of Self-Government (subject to the reservation of certain powers to the Governor) under a Constitution introduced by Letters Patent dated 5th September 1947. On the resignation of the Government led by Mr. D. Mintoff on the 24th April 1958, the Leader of the Opposition Dr. G. Borg Olivier refused to form a Government without a mandate from the electorate; disturbances followed, a state of emergency was declared on 30th April and the direct administration of the Island was assumed by the Governor. On the 15th April 1959, after abortive talks with Maltese leaders (November-December 1958) the state of emergency was brought to an end, the 1947 Constitution was revoked and replaced by an interim Constitution established by the Malta (Constitution) Order in Council, 1959, and the Malta Royal Instructions, 1959.

Under this Constitution all legislative and administrative powers were vested in the Governor acting, except in certain specified cases, in consultation with the Executive Council. The Executive Council was composed of three ex officio members (Chief Secretary, Legal Secretary, Financial Secretary), three other nominated official members, and four nominated unofficial members.

Following the report of a Constitutional Commission under the chairmanship of Sir Hilary Blood, a new Constitution for Malta was introduced by the Malta (Constitution) Order in Council, 1961, under which the Island is now known as "the State of Malta". The Constitution provides inter alia for the protection of fundamental rights and freedoms of the individual; the United Kingdom Government retains responsibility for defence and external affairs (however, in August 1962, the Government of Malta was granted the power to negotiate directly with the "Six" for the eventual association of Malta with
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

the Common Market and to deal directly with other countries for the purpose of obtaining financial and technical aid). The Governor and Commander-in-Chief is required to consult with the Cabinet composed of a Prime Minister and seven Maltese Ministers, in exercising all his powers, with certain exceptions; a United Kingdom Commissioner is appointed to represent the Government of the United Kingdom in Malta. A Consultative Council is also appointed composed of the Governor as Chairman, the Prime Minister, the United Kingdom Commissioner, three members appointed by a Secretary of State and three members appointed by the Prime Minister, providing for consultation on policy relating to defence and external affairs, and on any matters affecting the relations between the United Kingdom Government and the Government of Malta as may be referred to it by either Government.

Elections for the fifty seats in the Legislative Assembly, as opposed to forty seats under the 1947 Constitution were held on 17th, 18th and 19th February 1962. The state of the parties in the Legislative Assembly now is: Nationalist Party twenty-six seats, Malta Labour Party sixteen seats, Christian Workers Party four seats, Democratic Nationalist Party three seats, Progressive Constitutional Party one seat. The Nationalist Party Leader Dr. Giorgio Borg Olivier, was entrusted with the formation of a Government and the Cabinet was sworn in on the 5th March 1962.
Land Policy
There is no restriction on the alienation of land to non-Maltese but the Mortmain Law restricts the alienation of land to religious bodies or institutions under certain circumstances.
Taxation
The principal forms of taxation are income tax, import duties, excise duties, stamp duties, entertainment duties, tombola duty, licence duties and succession and donation duties.
Income tax, introduced in 1949, is levied on all personal incomes, with certain exceptions, at the following rates :—
2s. for every £ of the first £200.
3s. for every £ of the next £300.
4s. 6d. for every £ of the next £400.
6s. 6d. for every £ of the remainder.
The tax on companies is at the rate of 5s. on every £ of the chargeable income.
A surtax is also levied on incomes exceeding £2,500 at the following rates:—
2s. for every £ of the first £500.
3s. for every £ of the next £500.
4s. for every £ of the next £500.
5s. for every £ of the remainder.
Personal deductions :—

Single persons .. .. 240
Husband and wife .. .. 420
Children .. .. .. 80 per child.
There are also deductions for dependants and allowances in respect of contributions to pensions funds, insurance policies and medical expenses.

Interest at 2½% is paid by the Government on £78,281 outstanding balance on 31st March 1962 of the debt known as "Massa Frumentaria" incurred by the Municipality of Valletta under the Government of the Order of St. John of Jerusalem, in connexion with the monopoly of the
MALTA
wheat trade. The funds of the "Massa Frumentaria" were in 1798 appropriated by the French Government then in occupation of the Island.

Under the Malta Treasury Bills Emergency Ordinance, 1958, the Government may raise up to £1,500,000 by the issue of Treasury Bills. The amount of such bills outstanding on 31st March 1962 was £1,028,000.

The Government is also empowered to raise up to £4,000,000 under the Local Loans (Registered Stock and Securities) Ordinance, 1959. Three issues of Local Development Registered 6% Stock have been made under this Ordinance. The first of £1,300,000 was made at £98% in December 1959, the second of £1,200,000 at £95% in October 1960, and the third of £1,670,000 at £95% in November 1961. All issues were fully subscribed.
Currency

The currency of Malta consists of notes of £5, £1 and 10s., issued by the Board of Commissioners of Currency, together with British coins, including silver, cupro-nickel and bronze coins and local notes in denominations of 5s., 2s. 6d., 2s. and 1s. Bank of England notes ceased to be legal tender in the Island in 1949. Maltese £5, £1 and 10s. notes are on a par with sterling and £ for £ coverage is maintained at the Bank of England. The amount of local currency in circulation on 31st March 1962 was £23,541,000.
Development Plans
During the financial year ended 31st March 1962, a total of £3,845,009 was spent on development and other capital works.

Since the end of the War, direct aid by way of free grant from Her Majesty's Government to Malta has amounted to over £52 million, mainly in respect of capital expenditure including War Damage and Reconstruction grants.

The Five-Year Development Plan for the Maltese Islands covering the period 1959-64, which was published in October 1959, was reviewed towards the beginning of the third year. The Reviewed Plan was published in July 1961 and provides for an expenditure of over £351 million of which £291 million was made available by Her Majesty's Government in loans (£12.48 m.) and grants (£16.77 m.), including £191 million from Colonial Development and Welfare Funds. It was also anticipated to secure a long term loan of £2.3 million from the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development to finance in part the cost of a new power station and distillation plant.

The Plan aims at diversifying the Maltese economy which has hitherto been largely sustained by the expenditure of the fighting Services; this expenditure is bound to decline because of changing defence policies and concepts. The measures contemplated in the Plan to achieve this end may be summarized as follows:—

(a) The Harbour, which is Malta's leading natural asset, is to be developed by the conversion of the Dockyard to commercial use and by the construction of modern deep water quays and other port facilities. A £71 million loan from Colonial Development and Welfare sources is being made for the Dockyard operation which has been entrusted to a United Kingdom firm of ship repairers. The new quays have been completed. The completion date of the few outstanding works is March 1963.

(b) Funds are provided for the building of a fully serviced industrial estate, work on which is now well advanced, and it is expected that some seventeen factories will have been built by March 1963. Capital loans and grants are also offered to industry, in addition to an income tax "holiday" and Customs duties easements available under the Aids to Industries Ordinance. An extensive publicity campaign undertaken by an Aids to Industries Board has drawn the attention of industrialists in the United Kingdom, on the Continent and elsewhere to the wide range of aid offered. The results have been satisfactory and some 25 projects for different industries have been approved. Besides these direct aids, the Plan also sets out to provide the basic services necessary for industrialization apart from the port already referred to above, i.e., the expansion of the water and electricity supplies and the modernization and expansion of the telephone services. Technical Education is also to be developed in order to meet industry's increasing need for skills. The construction of a College of Arts, Science and Technology has started and a grant of £100,000 has been secured from U.N.E.S.C.O. towards furniture and equipment.

(c) The tourist industry is being built up to take advantage of Malta's climatic, scenic and historical assets. The Capital loans and grants to industry referred to above will also be made available for hotels. The extension of essential services to hotel resort sites is being undertaken by the Government and for this purpose just under £1,000,000 is being provided.
(d) The two indigenous industries, agriculture and fisheries, are being assisted towards greater efficiency.

(e) Although the greater share of the funds is allocated to these economic objectives, substantial provision is made for the social services—primary and secondary education, health, housing, sewers and public buildings.
Education

Compulsory education was introduced in 1946. Primary education is carried on principally in free Government schools which are Roman Catholic. There are 112 Primary schools, where Maltese and English are the only languages of instruction and where Italian and French have recently been included in the curriculum, with an enrolment of 51,885 pupils; and six Grammar schools with 3,676 pupils. The Royal University of Malta, founded in 1592 and raised to University status in 1769, has an average attendance of 350 students and grants degrees and diplomas in divinity, law, medicine, architecture, engineering, dental surgery, pharmacy, arts, science and public administration. The number of Maltese studying in United Kingdom Universities or colleges during 1961 was 100. There are 172 evening classes for languages, technical and commercial subjects with an attendance of 3,000 and 179 literacy classes for illiterates and semi-illiterates with an attendance of 857. There are three Secondary Technical Schools providing seven-year courses leading to G.C.E. Ordinary and Advanced level for 1,660 pupils of whom 593 are girls. A Technical Institute is being built to provide three to five year craft and technician courses for 725 pupils between 14 and 17 years. This Institute is to incorporate the various technical schools which are spread over different areas. An apprenticeships school provides five-year courses for 356 apprentices on the day release system. A new College of Arts,
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

Science and Technology has provided full-time and part-time courses in Engineering at the technician level for 90 students in its first session. St. Patrick's Residential School has 117 boarders, age 9 to 15. The School of Art provides seven evening classes attended by 185 persons of all ages. 212 persons are undergoing accelerated full-time technical training in various trades, at two Government Industrial Training Centres in Malta and one in Gozo. These centres were started under the vocational training scheme for prospective migrants. There is a Government home for girls with an average total of 54 inmates. The total number of private (primary and secondary) schools is 86 with an attendance of about 18,833 pupils.
In 1961–62 ordinary expenditure on education was £1,885,875; capital expenditure was £504,818. Expenditure on education in 1935 was £149,521.
Libraries, Museums, etc.

The Royal Library including the Juvenile Library has a stock of 66,700 works, the Gozo Public Library has 17,100. There are also 48 district circulating libraries in the various towns and villages (35 in Malta and 13 in Gozo), with a total book-stock of 84,950.

The pre-historic, historic and artistic heritage of the Islands is cared for by the Museum Department which continually adds to present knowledge in all three fields through excavation and research. The following Museums and monuments are open to visitors:—
Malta:—

The National Museum, Valletta. Ghar Dalam, the Cave and Museum, Birzebbuga. Hal Saflieni Prehistoric Hypogeum, Paola. Tarxien Prehistoric Temples, Tarxien. Hagar Qim Prehistoric Temples, near Qrendi. Mnajdra Prehistoric Temples, near Qrendi. Museum of Roman Antiquities and Roman Villa, Rabat. St. Paul's Catacombs, Rabat. The Palace Armoury, Valletta.
Gozo:—
The Gozo Museum, The Citadel, Victoria. Ggantija Prehistoric Temples, Xaghra.
Health

There are seven Government hospitals in Malta with a total of 2,600 beds and five in Gozo with 456 beds. During 1961 in-patients numbered 14,111, out-patient attendances amounted to 94,317. There are in addition 42 District Medical Officers, 48 Health Visitors (district nurses) and five subsidized Midwives. In the Government dispensaries the number of attendances by the public in 1961 was 95,672.
The principal infectious diseases during 1961 were:—

Vaccination of all infants against smallpox is compulsory on attaining the age of two months.

Communications

The international airport of Malta is Luqa Airfield, which is also a Royal Air Force Station. There are other Service airfields at Hal Far and Ta' Qali. British European Airways operate the following scheduled services:—(a) Twelve services a week to London. These call at Palermo or Naples or Alghero or at Rome; (b) three services a week to Catania; and (c) five services a week to Benghazi via Tripoli.

Skyways Limited, under a pooling agreement with British European Airways operate a scheduled service between Malta and London direct twice weekly. British United Airways operate freight services between Malta and East, Central and South Africa, and between Malta, Frankfurt and London.
During 1961, 2,635 aircraft offloaded 53,165 passengers and picked up 55,503 others. 33,892 passengers passed through Malta in transit.
The size of Malta does not provide scope for internal air services and the only railway line ceased to operate in 1931.

Twelve miles of roads have been resurfaced with hot-laid bitumen macadam. Surfacing for main roads remains penetration macadam (semi-grouting) and 479 miles are paved in this method using limestone aggregate and bituminous emulsion; 190 miles of country and estate roads are paved in water-bound macadam.
The number and tonnage of ships which entered the Grand Harbour during 1961 are:—
2,032 Steamships, tonnage 3,853,803.
140 Sailing ships, tonnage 8,994.

There is no regular passenger line between Malta and the United Kingdom. The Italian Tirrenia Line runs a regular passenger service between Malta and the Continent calling at Syracuse, Catania and Naples. (Frequency: (1) July-September every three days; (2) October-June every 5 days.)
Broadcasting

Under the Broadcasting Ordinance, 1961, broadcasting services in Malta were made the responsibility of the Malta Broadcasting Authority, established on 29th September 1961. The Authority has entered into agreements with Rediffusion (Malta) Limited granting an extension of their existing licence to provide a wired sound service up to 1986, and with the Malta Television Service Limited (a new company constituted in Malta and within the Rediffusion group of Companies) to provide Television and Wireless Sound Services for a similar period. In the case of Wireless Sound, the company has been allowed a period of five years within which to decide whether or not to take up this concession: if it has not done so by then the concession will lapse.

Local television transmissions were inaugurated on 29th September 1962. The service is in English and Maltese and operates on the 625 line standard. In addition, Italian Television transmissions can be received.
Television sets number 18,500 with another 9,800 licences for wireless receiving sets; as on 31st July 1962 the subscribers to Rediffusion's wired sound service numbered 46,000.
MALTA
Principal Occupations

The gainfully-occupied population (89,220) was engaged in 1961 in the following occupations : agriculture (7,280), fishing (600), stone quarrying and clay pits (720), manufacturing and mechanical (15,260), construction (8,260), commerce (12,040), communications (4,080), community, recreation and personal services (8,480), Government (16,790), dockyard and Service Departments, including local servicemen (15,710).

On 30th June 1962 there were 62 registered trade unions with a total membership of 34,927, whereof 38 associations of employees with a membership of 30,915, 16 of employers with a membership of 1,854 and 8 combinations of employers and employees with a membership of 2,158.
Main Crops and Products
The main crops are potatoes, onions, grapes, wheat, barley and forages. Fresh vegetables and potatoes are the main cash crops.
The livestock population in 1961 was: Cattle 8,090; horses 1,906; donkeys 2,388; mules 1,599; sheep 11,603; goats 31,012; pigs 16,392; poultry 360,008; rabbits 53,295.
During 1961, 27,000 cwts. of fish were landed by local fishermen (mainly during the months between May and November) selling at a retail value of £331,000.
Malta has to import nearly all the raw materials required for industry.
The two leading industries are the ship-repair yard—formerly a Royal Naval establishment privately operated since April 1959—and the building industry.

Besides a number of light industries produce, flour, macaroni, edible oil, tomato paste, canned fruits and vegetables, beer, soft drinks, wine; buttons, footwear, gloves, ladies' handbags, knitwear, homespun lace, nylon socks and stockings, shirts and pyjamas, textiles, leather goods; cigarettes, pipes and other smoking requisites; candles, matches, paints, bituminous emulsion, mirrors, crown corks, nails and screws, neon-signs, refrigerators, boats, brooms; flowers and seeds.

Following the grant of assistance under the Aids to Industries Scheme, these new industries have been set up, namely: automobile assembly; bacon; carnation and chrysanthemum cuttings; cutting and polishing of rough diamonds; detergents; expanded plastics materials; hand-made jewellery; infants' wearing apparel; iron and steel products; knitwear; light engineering; marine paints; plastic articles; spring interior mattresses; synthetic rubber seals; synthetic yarn processing; textiles.
The Malta Trade Fair Corporation holds yearly International Exhibitions in July. In recent years the Malta Trade Fair has attracted the participation of various countries.
(a) Excluding gold bullion.

In 1938 45% of domestic exports went to the United Kingdom, 4% to other Commonwealth countries and 51% to foreign countries. The corresponding figures in 1961 were:—27%, 8%, 65%.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations
The marketing of agricultural produce is controlled by the Agricultural Produce (Marketing) Regulations 1952.

Fresh fruit and vegetables are sold by wholesale auctions under Government supervision at 6 Marketing Centres run by 23 "Pitkali" or middlemen and by Co-operative marketing societies. The former charge a commission of 6½% whilst the latter charge only 4-7/12%. Government makes a contribution to the Co-operative marketing societies at the rate of 3¼%.

In 1961 there were 13 registered agricultural producers co-operative societies, 10 of them specialising in marketing and represented by a central society. There were also 2 milk producers, 2 agricultural equipment, 1 wine producer and 1 consumer co-operative society. 3 other non-agricultural and 3 agricultural producer societies were in course of liquidation.

STATE OF SINGAPORE
Area
The area of Singapore, including inland waters and adjacent islets, is 224.5 square miles. The City of Singapore covers an area of 37.2 square miles.
Population of Singapore
The total population at the 1957 census was 1,445,929.
The estimated population as on 31st December 1961 was 1,712,600:

Malays* 240,200
Chinese .. 1,287,700
Indians and Pakistanis 142,900
Europeans 13,800
Eurasians 13,300
Others .. 14,700
*The racial group 'Malays' includes Indonesians.
Geographical Features
The island of Singapore is connected with the mainland of the Malay peninsula by a causeway
three-quarters of a mile long, carrying a road, railway and water mains.

The highest point on Singapore Island is Bukit Timah, 581 feet above sea level. The largest permanent stream is the Sungei Seletar which is nine miles long. Headwaters from this and two other streams have been impounded to form reservoirs.
Climate of Singapore

There are no well marked dry and wet seasons. Rain falls throughout the year, temperature is uniform, humidity high. The average annual rainfall is 96 inches; the average maximum temperature is $87^{\circ}$ , the average minimum $75^{\circ}$ ; the average maximum relative humidity is 95%, the average minimum 64%.
History

A prosperous commercial centre flourished at Singapore under the name of Singapura (the "Lion City") in the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries; this was destroyed by the Javanese in 1377. In 1819, Sir Stamford Raffles signed an agreement with the Sultan of Johore Lingga which gave the British the right to establish a factory, and under which the Sultan agreed that no land was to be alienated to, or treaty concluded with, any other foreign power. The Island's population, 150 when Raffles landed in January 1819, had risen by August to 5,000 and the Settlement developed rapidly into a trading centre. By the Treaty of London, 1824, Holland withdrew its former objections to the occupation of Singapore and ceded Malacca, while Britain surrendered Bencoolen and all the East India Company's possessions in Sumatra. In the same year a treaty was signed with the Sultan by which he ceded to the East India Company the island of Singapore in full sovereignty and property. By this time Raffles had endowed the island with a magistracy, a code of laws and police force, trading regulations, a town-planning scheme and an English school (the Raffles Institution). From 1824 the phenomenal progress of Singapore continued. The value of trade in 1864 was £13,252,175. The population had risen by 1860 to 81,734 of all nationalities, but with a significant majority (over 50,000) of Chinese. Singapore had completely overshadowed its sister Settlements of Malacca and Penang, with which it had been incorporated in 1826 as the Straits Settlements, and it was natural that the seat of Government should be transferred from Penang to Singapore in 1832.

The Straits Settlements was brought under the Presidency of Bengal in 1830 and transferred to the direct control of the Governor-General in 1851. With the abolition of the Company's monopoly of the China trade in 1834 India lost interest in the Straits: it was difficult to find suitable officials for the territory and protection in war was impossible. The Viceroy, Lord Canning, had no objection to the transfer of the Settlements to the Colonial Office, which in 1860 was agreed to in principle. In 1866 an Act was passed to transfer the control of the Settlements from the Indian Government to the Colonial Office. This was formally put into effect in 1867 when the Straits Settlements became a Crown Colony.

Though the adjacent islands of Blakang Mati and Pulau Brani had been fortified, the garrison in 1914 consisted only of the equivalent of two battalions, while from the naval point of view Singapore was no more than a port of call and
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

coaling station. With the emergence of Japan as the third naval power in the world the British Government decided in 1921 that Singapore should become the headquarters of a battle fleet and by 1938 a first-class naval base had been constructed with graving and floating docks to accommodate the largest capital ships. The defences of the island were, however, designed for resistance to attack by sea and in February 1942 it fell to a Japanese land attack down the Malay Peninsula and across the Johore Strait. For three and a half years it was occupied by the Japanese, but was liberated on the 5th September, 1945, without opposition, by forces under the command of Lord Louis (now Earl) Mountbatten, Supreme Allied Commander South-East Asia, and upon the restoration of civilian government, it was detached from the other Settlements of Penang and Malacca and placed under a separate administration.
Government
The first elections were held in April 1948 and only six of the 22 Legislative Council members were elected. In 1951 the number of elected members was increased by three.

The next milestone in Singapore's constitutional development was an Order in Council which came into force on 8th February 1955, and which gave Singapore a large measure of internal self-government. The form of Government was a Council of Ministers collectively responsible to a Legislative Assembly whose membership was increased to 32 members, 25 of whom were elected from single-member constituencies. The other seven members consisted of three official members and four nominated unofficials. The Governor ceased to preside over the Assembly and was replaced by a Speaker whom he appointed.
The Council of Ministers consisted of the Governor as President, three ex officio members and seven Ministers appointed from among the elected and nominated members of the Assembly.

Singapore achieved statehood in 1959 after 140 years of British rule. An agreement signed in London on 11th April 1957 provided for the constitution of a State of Singapore with full internal self-government, and the creation of a Singapore citizenship. There would be as the Queen's representative a Malayan-born Yang di-Pertuan Negara, or Head of State, a Cabinet presided over by the Prime Minister and a fully elected Legislative Assembly of 51 members presided over by a Speaker and Deputy Speaker of their own selection.

The new constitution under the Singapore (Constitution) Order in Council 1958, came into being on 3rd June 1959, by a proclamation of the Governor, Sir William Goode, who thereupon became concurrently Singapore's first Yang di-Pertuan Negara and the United Kingdom Commissioner. These two Offices were, however, after the first six months of the constitution, separated and held by different individuals. The Head of State must now be Malayan-born. The United Kingdom Commissioner remains responsible for external defence and external affairs but responsibility in certain fields of external affairs is delegated to the Government of the State of Singapore.

The Singapore Government is responsible for internal security, subject, however, to the oversight of an Internal Security Council consisting of three representatives each of the United Kingdom and Singapore Governments and one of the Govern-
t of the Federation of Malaya.

The Council of Ministers has been replaced by a Cabinet of nine elected Ministers presided over by the Prime Minister. The Ministers are collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly for policy but have full executive authority over their respective ministries, each of which has a Parliamentary Secretary (an elected Assemblyman), and a Permanent Secretary (a Civil Servant) who exercises supervision under the general direction of the Minister. There is a Deputy Prime Minister. The portfolios of the other Ministers are:—Labour, Finance, Health and Law, Culture, Home Affairs and Education, National Development.

Towards the end of 1961 the Governments of the Federation of Malaya and Singapore negotiated the heads of an agreement for a merger of the State of Singapore with the Federation of Malaya. These proposals provided that Singapore would be a State within the Federation, but on special conditions and with a larger measure of local autonomy than the other States forming the Federation. Defence, external affairs and security would be the responsibility of the Federation Government; education and labour in particular would be among the matters remaining within the responsibility of the Singapore Government. The proposals were published by the Singapore Government as a White Paper (Cmnd. 33, 1961).

Following talks in London in November 1961 the Prime Ministers of Britain and the Federation of Malaya issued a joint statement agreeing that the creation of a Federation of Malaysia, which would embrace the Federation of Malaya, Singapore, North Borneo, Sarawak and Brunei, was a desirable aim. The statement took note with satisfaction of the heads of agreement for the merging of the State of Singapore with the Federation. The text of the statement was published by the British Government as a White Paper (Cmnd. 1563).

Following further talks in London in July 1962 it was announced that the British and Malayan Governments had decided in principle that, subject to the necessary legislation, the proposed Federation of Malaysia should be brought into being by 31st August 1963. The two Governments stated their intention to give effect to this decision by concluding within six months a formal agreement which, among other things, would provide for the transfer of sovereignty in Singapore by 31st August 1963; the relationship between Singapore and the new Federation, as agreed between the Governments of Malaya and Singapore; and defence arrangements as set out in Cmnd. 1563.
Land Policy

Alienation is now invariably by leases for periods up to 99 years. Statutory grants and grants in fee simple are issued only in special circumstances affecting old titles. The supply of vacant State land in the Island is practically exhausted and large areas have had to be acquired for public purposes, such as roads, school sites, housing and particularly for industrial development.
Taxation

Income tax is levied on income accruing in or derived from the State or received in the State from outside. Companies are chargeable at 40% and resident individuals at a sliding scale ranging from 5% on the first $1,500 of chargeable income to 55% on incomes exceeding
SINGAPORE

$100,000. Non-resident individuals are charged at 40%, but British subjects and British protected persons may be entitled to a proportion of the personal allowances and reduced rates applicable to resident individuals. Double taxation arrangements with the United Kingdom, Sweden, Denmark, Norway and Japan are in force.

Personal allowances are $2,000 for an unmarried person and $3,000 for a married couple, with a sliding scale of allowances for children from $750 for the first child to $200 for the sixth and subsequent children up to a maximum allowance of $6,150 for the family. Earned income relief is 20%, subject to a maximum of $1,000. In cases where children are maintained and educated outside Malaya, the allowances for children may be increased up to double the amounts. Deductions are also allowed in respect of life assurance premia and contributions to approved pension or provident funds.
In 1962 legislation was introduced to enable married women to be assessed separately in respect of their earned income.
In 1961, 28.4% of revenue was derived from Customs and Excise and 63.75% from Income and other Taxes.

Currency

The currency in circulation is that issued by the Board of Commissioners of Currency, Malaya and British Borneo, and is the only legal tender in the Federation of Malaya, Singapore, Sarawak, North Borneo and Brunei. It consists of the following :
(i) Currency Notes issued by
(a) The Board of Commissioners of Currency, Malaya ;
(b) The Board of Commissioners of Currency, Malaya and British Borneo.
(ii) Nickel coin of 5 cent denomination.
(iii) Cupro-nickel coin of various denominations below $1.
(iv) Copper coin of 1 cent denomination and below.
(v) Bronze coin of 1 cent denomination and below.

(i) above consists of notes of the denominations of $10,000, $1,000, $100, $50, $10, $5, $1 and subsidiary notes of 50 cents, 20 cents, 10 cents, 5 cents and 1 cent. Currency notes bearing dates prior to 1st July 1941 were demonetized on 31st August 1948 and silver coins on 31st December 1952 and ceased to be legal tender from these dates.
The currency is linked to sterling and is fixed at 2s. 4d. to the dollar. The total amount of currency notes in circulation on 31st December 1961 was $1,131,579,796.22 and coins $56,814,705.33.
Development Plans
The first Development Plan for the State of Singapore published in April 1961 and approved by the Legislative Assembly, covers the period 1961-1964.

The Plan covers the entire public sector comprising all Government Ministries and statutory bodies (the City Council, the Singapore Harbour Board, the Singapore Telephone Board and the Singapore Housing and Development Board). Approved estimates for development expenditure for Government and statutory bodies are incorporated in the Plan.

Estimated capital expenditure on development projects in the planned period amounts to $871 million. The proportions in which resources are to be allocated between developing economic services and developing social services have been estimated. The Plan emphasises projects conducive to industrial expansion while recognising the need to expand social services to meet the rapid population growth. Approximately 58% of the planned expenditure would be on projects directly contributing to economic development and 41% on projects for social development and improvement of public administration. The major economic development projects are the establishment of the Economic Development Board with a capital of $100 million, the expansion of electricity, gas and water supply which require more than $146 million and the development of industrial estates for which $90 million has been allocated. Other major projects include further development of harbour facilities, the development of Singapore Airport and the improvement of the entire transport and communications system to meet Singapore's growing needs. Total planned expenditure for all transport and communications projects would amount to about $117 million.
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Out of the total of $350 million planned for development of social services $284 million is allocated to health, education and housing.

The extensive housing programme requiring $154 million has been formulated in recognition of the fact that past programmes have been on too small a scale to be an effective solution to Singapore's housing problems.

In recent years, estimates of capital formation indicated that the rate of capital formation has been in the region of 10% of the gross national income. It is the aim of the Plan to increase the rate to some 20% of the gross national income, this being estimated to be necessary if the needs of the growing population are to be adequately met.
Economic Development Board

The Economic Development Board (E.D.B.), after the initial months of organization in 1961, became fully operational by the end of the year. In 1962 it began an intensive investment promotion programme locally and abroad to attract new industries to Singapore and to expand capacity or range of production where plants already existed. A liaison office was set up in Japan and representatives were appointed in Australia and Hong Kong to deal expeditiously with enquiries from these countries on prospects of industrial investment in Singapore.
Industrial Development

Industry feasibility studies were undertaken and investment reports prepared indicating what potentially profitable lines of manufacture exist in a wide variety of industries—chemical, electrical, metal working, building materials and food fields, and in ship repairing.
Jurong Industrial Town

The work on the vast 9,000 acre new industrial satellite town at Jurong on the south-west of Singapore Island started in the middle of 1961. The town will have road and rail access, a deep water harbour for bulk transportation of industrial raw materials, coastal and lighter wharves, low cost housing for factory employees and all the utility services. Development proceeded at a rapid pace and the first sites for industries were allocated in September 1962. Phase I development at Jurong, to be completed by the end of 1963, will provide 1,100 acres of industrial sites, divided into a light industry zone, a heavy and general industry zone and a special zone for industries requiring special handling facilities, etc.

Work has started for the construction of a steel rolling mill at Jurong which will have an ultimate capacity of 60,000 tons per annum. Raw materials for this mill will be steel scrap from ships which are being broken on site at Jurong. Another major industry, located in Jurong, will be the new joint Singapore-Japanese shipyard which will be built on an off-shore island. Other industries will include plants producing plywood, calcium carbide, caustic soda and animal feedmix; the building of pleasure craft. It is also expected that brickworks, sawmills and a grain silo for bulk handling of grains will be built.
Other Industrial Estates
Besides Jurong, factory sites for small scale and light industries are also available at the Redhill Industrial Estate, the Tanglin Halt Industrial Estate, the Bendemeer Road Industrial Estate and

the Kampong Ampat Industrial Estate. A total of 72 acres of sites are available, divided into lots for the construction of standard factories, and lots on which buildings to suit the individual needs of industries can be built.

The Redhill Industrial Estate has been completely allocated and industries to be set up there include plants manufacturing zip fasteners, paper products, glass goods, naphthalene balls and tablets, tin cans, printing ink, groundnut oil, animal feed and office equipment. Applications for sites on the other small industrial estates are heavy.
Technical Consulting Service

As part of its functions, the Board, through its Technical Consultants Service, provides technical advice to industries when required. Part of this service was to establish an Industrial Research Unit, to be operational before the end of 1962, which would undertake research of a technological nature for industries.
Industrial Financing

As an Industrial Development Bank, the Board is prepared to provide long term financing for the establishment of new industries or the expansion of existing ones. Up to September 1962 the Board approved loans totalling over M$.10 million. These loans were made to industries engaged in oil refining, printing, flour milling, manufacture of noodles, steel rolling, manufacture of metal tubes and ship breaking.
Education

The education system of the State of Singapore provides education for some 401,587 pupils in 568 regular schools. There are 12,778 teachers employed, 47.73% of whom are being trained. Of the school pupils, approximately 18.44% are in secondary classes.

The net cost of education in 1961 was $70.8 million. The comparative figures for 1948 of $4.725 million indicate the vast increase in cost due to the implementation of the State's Development programme in education.
The University of Singapore has 1,561 men and 573 women students, of whom 727 are from outside Singapore.
In the current academic year (1962–63) there are 2,110 students attending Nanyang University's three colleges of Arts, Science and Commerce.

The Singapore Polytechnic has in the current academic year 2,735 students attending classes in four Departments: Accountancy; Nautical; Engineering; Building and Architecture. 1,117 are full-time students, 291 are students released by their employers to attend one full day and two evenings per week and the remainder are evening students.
Libraries, Museums, etc.

The National Library contains about 170,000 volumes, including a large and important collection of books on Malaya. There are two branches. Books for children are provided at each library, and there is a separate children's room at the Central Library. The library functions as a free public library and is financed by the Government. It serves as the depository for the State's archives and for the books and other publications which are printed in Singapore and received under the terms of the Printers and Publishers Ordinance.
Digitized by Google
SINGAPORE

Books in Chinese, Malay and Tamil, which languages are currently spoken in Singapore are now available, as well as books in English and music scores. A mobile library service to schools in the rural areas is in operation and will later be extended to serve the adult population in the rural areas.

A new library building was completed and occupied by the end of 1960. Its facilities include large adult and children's lending libraries, an air-conditioned reference library, stacks and study carrels, and conference, exhibition and lecture halls.

The National Museum (formerly known as the Raffles Museum) contains collections covering the zoology, ethnography and archaeology of the Malaysian region, and of the history of Singapore and Malaya. It publishes the Bulletin of the National Museum (devoted to papers on zoology), the Memoirs of the National Museum (devoted to papers on local history and anthropology) and a series of popular pamphlets on museum subjects.
The Malayan Branch of the Royal Asiatic Society, closely associated with the National Museum, publishes a quarterly journal devoted mainly to papers on history, archaeology and ethnography.
Health

The Medical and Health services of the State of Singapore are now completely unified on an island-wide basis under the administration of the Ministry of Health with the integration of all the functions of the City Health Department, the City Cleansing Department and the Markets and Hawkers Department. The Ministry of Health now comprises 4 Divisions as follows:—
Headquarters Division
Hospitals Division
Health Division
Chemistry Division.
The Headquarters Division is responsible for the formulation of health policies and co-ordination of the activities of the other Divisions.

The Hospitals Division controls 11 hospitals which have a total of 6,805 beds. There are two hospitals for acute cases which have 1,647 beds; one hospital for infectious diseases (250 beds); one for obstetrics and gynaecology (438 beds); one for tuberculosis (1,234 beds); one for mental diseases and mental defection (1,915 beds); one for leprosy (1,823 beds); venereal diseases (61 beds); for chronic sick (120 beds) and a children's orthopaedic hospital with 120 beds. There are 27 static dispensaries, 9 travelling dispensaries and 2 floating dispensaries.

The Public Health Division is responsible for preventive services and this broadly includes Environmental Health, Quarantine and Epidemiology, Maternal and Child Health, School Health, Training and Health Education. The Environmental Health Service maintains effective control of general sanitation, food and drugs and infectious diseases. Singapore did not witness any dangerous infectious disease, viz. smallpox, plague, cholera, typhus fever, yellow or relapsing fever from 1960–1961 in spite of its proximity to territories in which these diseases are either epidemic or endemic. Towards the end of 1961, cholera epidemic broke out in the Borneo Territories, the Philippines, Hong Kong and Macao. A cholera inoculation campaign was organized and conducted on an island-wide basis and a total of 474,224 persons were inoculated.
A constant vigil is kept on the prevention of malaria and Singapore has been free from indigenous malaria for the past 10 years.

The Maternal and Child Health Service operates a network of major clinics, visiting centres and midwife stations throughout the city and rural areas. There are 20 main clinics in the rural district and 7 in the City. In addition there are 22 visiting centres and 8 village midwife centres. The service to mothers consists of ante-natal and post-natal care, a Domiciliary Midwifery Service, a Domiciliary After-Care service. The facilities made available for infant care have now been extended to children up to the school-going age. Services include preventive and curative work. A comprehensive immunisation programme is offered to all children. This includes vaccination against smallpox, immunisation against diphtheria, whooping cough and tetanus, and B.C.G. vaccination and poliomyelitis immunisation with sabin vaccine. The Ministry of Health's development programme provides in the next 3 years for the establishment in the city and rural areas of at least 5 new main centres and 10–12 midwife centres.

The School Health Service is centrally administered and functions at the Institute of Health. The service is responsible for the prevention of diseases and the promotion and maintenance of health among school children. There are 4 school clinics which deal with follow-ups from the routine school examination. Travelling dispensaries also visit school children in the more remote rural areas.

There is a Medical Faculty in the University of Singapore where the qualification of M.B., B.S. registrable in the United Kingdom can be obtained after a full course of training lasting six years. Training is also given for the B.D.S. (Bachelor of Dental Surgery) also registrable in the United Kingdom. A course for the degree of B.Ph. (Bachelor of Pharmacy) has been instituted. A nine-month course for the Diploma of the Royal Society of Health for Public Health Inspectors and a one-year course for the Health Visitor's Certificate of this Society is conducted at the Institute of Health. The University awards a Diploma in Public Health which is recognised by the General Medical Council.

The School of Nursing provides general nurse-training; associated schools provide specialized training for tuberculosis nurses, psychiatric nurses, paediatric nurses, theatre sister nursing, district nurses and public health nurses. The Assistant Nurse Training School and the Midwifery School provide training for Assistant Nurses and Midwives.
The training of public health inspectors is carried out at the Institute of Health and is one of the oldest courses of training in Singapore—having been started in 1921.
Statutory Boards control the qualification and registration of medical practitioners, dentists, pharmacists, nurses and midwives.

Health figures for the State continue to be satisfactory. The crude death rate is 5.94 per 1,000 in 1961 compared with 19.51 in 1939 and 8.10 in 1955. The infant mortality rate is 32.3 per 1,000 live births in 1961 compared with 130.4 in 1939 and 49.5 in 1955. The birth rate is 35.5 per 1,000, representing approximately 59,900 births.
The Blood Transfusion Service, run on a voluntary system, serves all Hospitals; approximately 1,200 transfusions are given each month.

The Government dental service provides free dental care to school-children, expectant and nursing mothers, the chronic sick and the poorer sections of the public at nominal charges. During 1962 five new dental clinics were opened, bringing the total number to 34. Attendances at all clinics total 260,781. The new Dental Nurses Training School at the Institute of Health is now functioning and the first batch of student nurses will graduate at the end of 1963. When it is fully functioning, a steady stream of Dental Nurses at the rate of 20 per year will be recruited into the service.
Inspections of dangerous and hazardous materials are carried out by the Chemistry Division.
Communications

Air—Singapore Airport, seven and a half miles from the city centre, is an international airport, and one of the finest in South-East Asia. It has a runway which has now been expanded to 9,000 feet (L.C.N. 77) with a wide taxi-way and efficient lighting. The Joint Air Traffic Control Centre based at Singapore Airport controls all flights within the confines of its 750,000 square miles Flight Information Region.
In 1960 a total of 14,492 air transport movements and 400,178 passengers used the airport. In addition there were 25,471 and 627 Training and Miscellaneous movements respectively.
Major external international passenger, air mail and cargo air carriers operating through Singapore are :
Qantas Empire Airways.
British Overseas Airways Corporation.
K.L.M. Royal Dutch Airlines.
Pan American World Airways.
Cathay Pacific Airways.
Thai Airways International.
Garuda Indonesian Airways.
Air Ceylon.
Air India International.
Japan Air Lines.

Malayan Airways Limited operate daily domestic flights to all important points in the Federation of Malaya and Borneo, and regular scheduled services to Indonesia, Thailand, Borneo, Sarawak and Hong Kong.
Road —The Public Works Department maintains 488 miles of bituminous roads in the City and Rural areas.

Rail —The Malayan Railway system is owned by the Government of the Federation of Malaya, and serves Singapore also. The track is of metre gauge and the total route mileage is 1,028 miles. The Railway enters the State at the Johore Causeway and continues for a distance of 16 miles to the terminal station near Keppel Harbour.
Water —The control of the port of Singapore is divided between (a) a government and (b) a non-government authority.

(a) The Master Attendant administers all the waters of the port including the Outer and Inner Roads, Keppel Harbour, the Western Anchorages, Telok Ayer Basin, the Singapore, Rochore, Kallang and Geylang rivers, etc.
(b) The Singapore Harbour Board administers the wharves, docks and premises of the Board situated in Keppel Harbour and a

slipway at Tanjong Rhu, under the provisions of the Ports Ordinance; also the quays and waterfront premises at Telok Ayer Basin, on behalf of the Singapore Government. The Chairman and members of the Board are appointed by the Yang di-Pertuan Negara.
The natural harbours around the southern end of the island still retain their deep water channel capable of taking the largest ships. Wharves comprise :

During 1961 a total of 39,190 vessels of 76,913,945 net registered tons entered and cleared the Port; this was an all-time record as to tonnage; 18.3 million tons of cargo were handled during the year.
The Board maintains a fleet of tugs for the towing, berthing and unberthing of ships; also a Fire Float.
The Board also maintains a fully equipped Fire Brigade specialising in ships' fires, and an efficient Police Force.

The Board has first-class facilities for dry docking and repairing ships of all sizes. There are six dry docks, the largest of these being the King's Dock, which can accommodate ships up to 45,000 tons gross. Repair berths total 3,000 feet in length.

The port is served by passenger and cargo lines from Europe, North and South America, South and East Africa, Australia, New Zealand and the various countries in Asia. Several coasting lines are engaged in conveying transit cargo to and from Thailand, Indonesia, Borneo, Sarawak, Malayan ports and Cambodia.
Broadcasting

Radio Singapore is operated by the Broadcasting Division of the Ministry of Culture. It is the largest publicity medium in the State of Singapore, operating a full time radio service in Chinese (seven dialects), English, Malay and Tamil. Its programmes are broadcast on the medium-wave band and the short-wave band. Four channel transmissions are used: one carries Chinese programmes, one carries English programmes, one is shared by Malay and Tamil programmes and one carries a new service—the "Suara Singapura" (Voice of Singapore) programme. The object of the new service is to promote the national language and, at the same time, to foster the growth of a Malayan culture amongst the various races and communities in Singapore.

The total number of hours of broadcasting average 389½ per week, of which approximately 246 hours are occupied by programmes originating from the Commercial Service, and about 38 hours are devoted to broadcasts for schools in Chinese, English, Malay and Tamil.
Radio Singapore has its studios and offices at Caldecott Hill and its main transmitting station at Jurong, some 14½ miles from the city. There are
SINGAPORE
eight transmitters at this station which are on the air daily. The programmes of Radio Singapore cover every aspect of community service and entertainment.
The Commercial service is potentially the largest service of its kind in Asia, with sponsor time-slots in three channels (four languages).

The activities of Radio Singapore include the National Music Library, the National Radio Archives which comprise all historical programmes dating back to 1945, and the presentation and technical operation of newscasts including news bulletins and news talks in all four languages.
The number of wireless receiving licences in Singapore at the end of July 1961 was 126,182.

A commercial company which operates a wired broadcasting service in Singapore under government licence had 49,325 subscribers in Singapore at 31st July 1961. Some of Radio Singapore's programmes, including news bulletins, are relayed daily.

Plans are in hand for the establishment of a Television Service, and it is hoped to commence the service on a small scale early in 1963. When established, the service will provide transmissions in Band III. It will be ultimately partially Commercial.
The TV Standard to be adopted is the C.C.I.R. 625-Lines System.
Principal Occupations

The estimated numbers of employees in the chief industries are: transport and communications (including port and lighterage) 45,300, commerce and finance 116,200, light industries 79,200, agriculture (largely market gardening) 26,000, public administration (central defence services and local authorities) 117,400. There are 124 registered trade unions of employees, having a membership of 164,462.
Main Products

The small area of agricultural land is used mainly to produce food crops for local consumption. Poultry-rearing as a rural industry is thriving and 18,000,000 head are kept. This is more than sufficient for local consumption and allows some to be exported. The number of pigs in Singapore is 750,000.

Manufactures comprise a wide range of light industries. The traditional concentration on the processing of primary products such as rubber, tin, rattan and the provision of ship-building, docking and ship facilities, light engineering works and motor assembly plants has been supplemented in recent years by a growing range of consumer goods such as dry cell batteries, glass products, edible oils, paper products, poultry feed, paints, metal boxes, shoe polish, confectionery, soap, pharmaceutical products and cement. Two refineries, one of which is completed and the other under construction, have been set up under the Pioneer Industries (Relief from Income Tax) Ordinance. The manufacture of cigarettes from imported tobacco is also being fairly extensively developed.

The huge long-term building programmes are also materially assisting construction industries such as brick and tile making, cement block manufacturing, saw-milling, metal window framework and manufacture of furniture.
Trade

Singapore handles most of the imports and exports of the Federation of Malaya. In addition much of the external trade of her immediate neighbours passes through her hands. The chief exports are rubber, tin, timber, petroleum products, copra, canned pineapples, coconut oil and spices. A wide range of manufactured consumer and capital goods is also imported for local consumption and for re-export.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

Singapore participates on a pan-Malayan basis with the Government of the Federation of Malaya in legislation concerning the marketing of rubber and pineapples. The Malayan Rubber Export Registration Board is concerned with regulating the packing, grading and shipping of rubber and with measures for ensuring that the quality of rubber to be exported is of the standard required. A Pan-Malayan Statutory Board, the Malayan Pineapple Industry Board, is vested with overall authority in the conduct of the affairs of the industry including the negotiation of agreements on prices and grades of fruits, the regulation of production, and the marketing of canned pineapples.

At the end of 1961, Singapore had 105 Co-operative Societies, made up of 43 Thrift and Loan Societies, 6 Employees' Credit Societies, 21 Thrift and Investment Societies, 13 Consumers' Societies, 7 Marketing Societies, 4 Rural Credit Societies, 2 Housing Societies, 2 Co-operative Banks, 1 Co-operative Union, and 6 Miscellaneous Societies. Total membership was 37,016, working capital $17,880,558 and Reserve Fund $855,516.
